svg.text: ZULU
GRAMMAR & NOUN CLASS SYSTEM
ZULU NOUN CLASSES
Zulu has 15 noun classes (Bantu gender system)
Each class has prefix: um-/aba- (people), i-/ama- (things), u-/o- (names)
umuntu "person" • abantu "people" (class 1/2)
Class determines verb agreement, adjective agreement, and pronouns
SUBJECT CONCORD SYSTEM
Verb agrees with subject noun class: prefix changes with class
Umuntu u-ya-dla "The person is eating" (um- class: u- concord)
Abantu ba-ya-dla "The people are eating" (aba- class: ba- concord)
Every noun class has unique subject, object, and possessive concords
ZULU VERB STRUCTURE
Verb = subject concord + tense marker + object concord + root + suffix
Ngi-ya-m-thanda "I love him/her" (ngi=I, ya=present, m=him/her)
Word order: SVO • but much encoded in verb prefixes
XHOSA GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
XHOSA NOUN CLASSES
Xhosa also has 15 noun classes; same Bantu system
umuntu "person" • abantu "people" (identical to Zulu!)
Most noun prefixes identical or near-identical between Zulu and Xhosa
XHOSA VERB SYSTEM
Identical structure to Zulu: concord + tense + object + root + suffix
Ndi-ya-m-thanda "I love him/her" (ndi=I in Xhosa vs. ngi=I in Zulu)
Key difference: Xhosa 1st person = ndi- • Zulu 1st person = ngi-
XHOSA VS ZULU: GRAMMAR DIFFERENCES
Xhosa: -ndi- (I) vs Zulu: -ngi- (I) — different 1st person prefix
Xhosa: ya- vs Zulu: ya- (same present tense marker)
Xhosa plural 2nd person: nin- • Zulu: ni- (near-identical)
MUTUAL INTELLIGIBILITY
Zulu and Xhosa are mutually intelligible at ~70-80% level
Grammar is so parallel that a Zulu speaker can learn Xhosa in weeks
Biggest differences: phonology (more Xhosa clicks) and vocabulary
PLATE 37c ALT · ZULU / XHOSA · GRAMMAR
